name: 'Abbás
summary: A youth and former servant who, under threat of torture, was coerced into walking the streets of Ṭihrán to identify Bábís for arrest and extortion during the 1852 persecution.
importance_reason: One-episode informer in the Ṭihrán persecution; memorable anecdote but incidental and one-named.

name: 'Abbás Mírzá
summary: A son of Muḥammad Sháh and half-brother of Náṣiri'd-Dín Sháh who, ambitious for power, sought Bahá'u'lláh's support for his political designs in Baghdád — overtures Bahá'u'lláh firmly condemned.
importance_reason: Qájár prince whose self-serving approach to Bahá'u'lláh is a named but minor episode.

name: 'Alí ibn Abí Ṭálib
summary: Cousin and son-in-law of the Prophet Muḥammad, first male believer in Islam, fourth Caliph and the first Imám of the Shí'ah; in Bahá'í writings He is one of the 'two witnesses' and His sayings are cited as foretelling Bahá'u'lláh.
importance_reason: Foundational Shí'ah holy figure, frequently invoked (30 mentions) in GPB as prophetic 'witness' to Bahá'u'lláh, but external to Bahá'í history proper.
characterizations: Al-Islam.org: 'Amir al-Mu'minin' (the Commander of the Faithful) is a title which is, according to Shiite belief, exclusively reserved for Imam Ali.
Al-Islam.org: He was the first of the family of the House and of the Companions to believe in God and His Apostle.

name: 'Azíz Khán-i-Mukrí, Sardár-i-Kull
summary: Qájár commander-in-chief (Sardár-i-Kull) under Náṣiri'd-Dín Sháh who, during a drunken revel, ordered the strangling of Ṭáhirih and her secret burial in the Ílkhání garden in 1852.
importance_reason: The official who ordered Ṭáhirih's execution — a recurring (13 mentions), historically taught antagonist of early Bábí history.
characterizations: Wikipedia: Aziz Khan Mokri was an Iranian military officer and grandee who occupied high offices under Naser al-Din Shah, serving as commander-in-chief of the army from 1853 to 1857.

name: A. L. M. Nicolas
summary: French orientalist and diplomat whose studies and translations of the Báb's writings (e.g. the 'Seven Proofs') and of Shaykhism are repeatedly cited as scholarly sources in The Dawn-Breakers.
importance_reason: Frequently cited (95 mentions) but purely as a bibliographic/scholarly source, not a historical participant.

name: Abel
summary: The biblical son of Adam slain by his brother Cain, invoked in GPB as a byword for primal fratricide when characterizing Covenant-breaking betrayals.
importance_reason: Scriptural figure used only as a rhetorical comparison; incidental.

name: Abraham
summary: The patriarch and Manifestation of God revered in Judaism, Christianity, and Islam; in Bahá'í history He is honored as a forebear of Bahá'u'lláh (through Katurah) and His banishment from Ur is cited as the historical prototype of Bahá'u'lláh's own exiles.
importance_reason: A Manifestation in the prophetic lineage and a literal ancestor of Bahá'u'lláh, though His role in this corpus is genealogical/typological rather than narrative.

name: Abraham Valentine Williams Jackson
summary: American orientalist and professor at Columbia University, cited in GPB among Western scholars whose attention attests the Faith's growing recognition.
importance_reason: One-mention Western academic listed as evidence of the Faith's progress.

name: Abram Simon
summary: American Reform rabbi cited in GPB among notable figures whose recognition attests the spread of the Faith.
importance_reason: One-mention name listed as evidence of growing recognition.

name: Abu'l-Qásim-i-Hamadání
summary: A Muḥammadan attendant who accompanied Bahá'u'lláh on His 1854 departure to the wilderness of Sulaymáníyyih; given money to act as a merchant, he was soon killed by thieves, leaving Bahá'u'lláh alone.
importance_reason: Named companion in the singular episode of Bahá'u'lláh's two-year retirement — a small but memorable narrative role.

name: Abu’l-Qásim (son of Karbilá’í Abú-Muḥammad)
summary: A Bábí, one of two sons of Karbilá'í Abú-Muḥammad, who together with Siyyid Aḥmad was brought before the prince during the Bábí episodes recounted in The Dawn-Breakers.
importance_reason: Minor named Bábí participant appearing in a single episode.

name: Abu’l-Qásim ‘Abdu’l-‘Aẓím
summary: A holy personage who, fleeing the Caliph Mutavakkil, lived in concealment at Rayy until his death c. 861 AD — mentioned for historical/geographic context in The Dawn-Breakers.
importance_reason: One-mention historical figure cited for background context, predating Bábí history.

name: Abu’l-Qásim, brother of Ḥusayn
summary: A Bábí named once in The Dawn-Breakers, identified only as the brother of Ḥusayn.
importance_reason: Bare one-mention name with no narrative detail.

name: Abu’l-Qásim-i-Ḵhurásání (keeper of the Tomb of Bahá’u’lláh)
aliases: Abu’l-Qásim-i-Ḵhurásání (keeper of the Tomb of Bahá’u’lláh)
summary: The 'brave-souled' keeper of the Shrine of Bahá'u'lláh at Bahjí who was forcibly expelled by Covenant-breakers c. 1921–22; on 'Abdu'l-Bahá's intervention British authorities restored the keys to him.
importance_reason: Named figure in the post-1921 struggle over the Holy Shrine — a small but specific role in the keys episode.

name: Abu’l-Qásim-ibn-i-Ḥájí-Zayná
summary: A Bábí who, with Akbar-ibn-i-'Ábid and others, died for his faith during the persecutions recounted in The Dawn-Breakers.
importance_reason: Martyr-list entry named once with no further detail.

name: Abu’l-Ḥasan ‘Alí ibn-i-Muḥammad Símarí
summary: The fourth and last of the 'Gates' (Abváb) who served as intermediaries to the Hidden Imám; he refused to name a successor, inaugurating the Greater Occultation — cited in The Dawn-Breakers as background to Bábí prophecy.
importance_reason: Shí'ah-history figure cited for context on the Gates of the Hidden Imám; appears to be the same person as the next record.

name: Abu’l-Ḥasan-‘Alí, the fourth Gate of the Hidden Imám
aliases: Abu’l-Ḥasan-‘Alí, the fourth Gate of the Hidden Imám
summary: The fourth Gate of the Hidden Imám who, asked before his death to name a successor, declined — ending the line of Gates; cited in The Dawn-Breakers as background to Shí'ah expectation.
importance_reason: Context figure for the Gates of the Hidden Imám; very likely the same person as 'Abu'l-Ḥasan ‘Alí ibn-i-Muḥammad Símarí' (possible duplicate to merge).

name: Abá-Baṣír
summary: A Bábí of Zanján, son of a fallen defender, who with Siyyid Ashraf was decapitated in that city; while kneeling in prayer he reportedly instructed his executioner how best to strike the blow.
importance_reason: Martyr with a memorable, often-recounted anecdote of fearless devotion, though appearing in few mentions.

name: Abú-Bakr
summary: The first Caliph of Islam, whose election in 632 AD began the Caliphate — cited in The Dawn-Breakers for historical background.
importance_reason: Major Islamic figure but cited only once here for chronological context, external to Bábí-Bahá'í history.

name: Abú-Jahl ‘Amr ibn Hishám
summary: A Meccan kinsman and arch-opponent of the Prophet Muḥammad (d. Battle of Badr, 624), invoked in GPB as the prototypical relative-turned-persecutor in a comparison to the Faith's enemies.
importance_reason: Islamic-history figure used once as a rhetorical comparison for hostility.

name: Abú-Ja‘far Muḥammad ibn-i-‘Uṯhmán
summary: The second of the four 'Gates' of the Hidden Imám, son of the first Gate — cited in The Dawn-Breakers as background to Shí'ah occultation doctrine.
importance_reason: One-mention context figure in the list of the Gates of the Hidden Imám.

name: Abú-Muslim al-Khurásání
summary: The 8th-century general whose Khurásání army overthrew the Umayyads and placed the 'Abbásids on the caliphal throne — cited in The Dawn-Breakers for the historical significance of Khurásán.
importance_reason: Major Islamic-history figure cited once for regional/historical background.

name: Abú-Muḥammad-i-Míyámay’í
summary: A Bábí from the village of Míyámay, named once in The Dawn-Breakers as a martyr.
importance_reason: One-mention martyr identified only by village.

name: Abú-‘Uṯhmán ‘Umar ibn-i-Sa‘íd al-‘Umarí
summary: The first of the four 'Gates' of the Hidden Imám, appointed by the eleventh Imám Ḥasan al-'Askarí — cited in The Dawn-Breakers as background to Shí'ah occultation doctrine.
importance_reason: One-mention context figure heading the list of the Gates of the Hidden Imám.

name: Adam
summary: The first man of biblical/Qur'ánic tradition, invoked in The Dawn-Breakers only as a temporal benchmark ('since the days of Adam') for the magnitude of the Bábí commotion.
importance_reason: Scriptural figure used once as a rhetorical time-marker.

name: Adham Bey
summary: An opponent of the Faith who fled to Egypt, was robbed en route, and ultimately had to beg assistance from the very Bahá'ís of Cairo — aid 'Abdu'l-Bahá directed them to give him.
importance_reason: One-episode adversary remembered chiefly for an illustrative anecdote of 'Abdu'l-Bahá's magnanimity.

name: Ahmed Djemal Pasha
summary: Ottoman military governor of Syria and member of the wartime Three Pashas triumvirate who threatened to crucify 'Abdu'l-Bahá and destroy Bahá'í properties in Palestine during WWI; the British Megiddo offensive under Allenby swept his forces away before harm came.
importance_reason: The last and gravest external threat to 'Abdu'l-Bahá at the World Centre — a recurring, historically taught antagonist (6 mentions).
characterizations: Wikipedia: ʻAbdu'l-Bahá was under threats from Cemal Paşa, the Ottoman military chief who at one point expressed his desire to crucify him and destroy Baháʼí properties in Palestine.
Wikipedia: An autocratic triumvirate of CUP Central Committee members led the Ottoman Empire, consisting of Talaat, Enver, and Ahmed Cemal (known as the Three Pashas).

name: Ahmed Şefik Midhat Pasha
summary: A reformist former Grand Vizier of the Ottoman Empire who invited 'Abdu'l-Bahá to Beirut; their contact is cited in GPB among the signs of the Faith's growing prestige.
importance_reason: Notable Ottoman statesman whose cordial contact with 'Abdu'l-Bahá is a recurring but minor point of recognition (3 mentions).

name: Ahriman
summary: The principle of evil (Destructive Spirit) in Zoroastrian cosmology, referenced in GPB in connection with the awaited savior who would triumph over him and usher in an age of peace.
importance_reason: Mythological/cosmological figure cited once for prophetic context.

name: Akbar-ibn-i-‘Ábid
summary: A Bábí who died for his faith alongside Abu'l-Qásim-ibn-i-Ḥájí-Zayná and others during the persecutions recounted in The Dawn-Breakers.
importance_reason: Martyr-list entry named once with no further detail.

name: Albert Basil Orme Wilberforce
summary: Anglican Archdeacon of Westminster who invited 'Abdu'l-Bahá to address the congregation of St. John the Divine and seated Him in the bishop's chair — cited in GPB as a mark of the Faith's recognition in the West.
importance_reason: Western cleric whose hosting of 'Abdu'l-Bahá is a small recurring detail (4 mentions) of His Western journeys.

name: Albert Dawson
summary: Editor of the Christian Commonwealth, cited once in GPB among Western figures associated with publicizing 'Abdu'l-Bahá's Western journeys.
importance_reason: One-mention Western editor named as evidence of recognition.

name: Aleksey Nikolayevich Kuropatkin
summary: Russian general and governor-general of Turkistán who, delegated by the Czar, attended the laying of the foundation stone of the Mashriqu'l-Adhkár of 'Ishqábád — cited once in GPB.
importance_reason: One-mention official noted for his ceremonial presence at the 'Ishqábád temple foundation.

name: Alexander Graham Bell
summary: The inventor of the telephone, named once in GPB among the eminent Western figures who met or honored 'Abdu'l-Bahá.
importance_reason: One-mention famous Westerner cited as evidence of recognition.

name: Alexander III
summary: Emperor of Russia (1881–1894), referenced in GPB in connection with the 'Ishqábád military court that punished the murderers of a Bahá'í and with his harsh domestic repression.
importance_reason: Sovereign mentioned (3×) for contextual political backdrop rather than direct involvement with the Faith.

name: Alma S. Knobloch
summary: An early American Bahá'í teacher who, following Dr. K. E. Fisher, established the Faith in Germany and carried it to Austria.
importance_reason: Pioneer who opened German-speaking Europe to the Faith — a meaningful but briefly noted teaching role (1 mention).

name: Amos
summary: The Hebrew prophet whose words ('The Lord will roar from Zion... the top of Carmel shall wither') are cited in GPB as foretelling the coming of Bahá'u'lláh.
importance_reason: Biblical prophet cited once for a prophecy applied to Bahá'u'lláh and Mount Carmel.

name: Amír Arslán Khán, son of the Sálár
common_name: Amír Arslán Khán (son of the Sálár)
aliases: Amír Arslán Khán, son of the Sálár
summary: A commander who, with Ja'far-Qulí Khán-i-Námdár, conducted operations against the Bábí defenders and displayed great cruelty — named once in The Dawn-Breakers; distinct from Amír Arslán Khán the Majdu'd-Dawlih.
importance_reason: One-mention military officer; a namesake distinct from the Majdu'd-Dawlih, kept separate by side/timeframe.

name: Amír Arslán Khán, the Majdu’d-Dawlih
summary: A maternal uncle of Náṣiri'd-Dín Sháh and governor of Zanján, described as 'envious and hypocritical' and named as a chief figure responsible for the 1852 Ṭihrán massacre of the Bábís.
importance_reason: Royal-kinsman official among those chiefly responsible for the great Ṭihrán persecution — recurring (7 mentions) named antagonist.

name: Andrew Carnegie
summary: The American steel industrialist and philanthropist, named once in GPB among eminent Western figures associated with 'Abdu'l-Bahá's journeys.
importance_reason: One-mention famous Westerner cited as evidence of recognition.
